item.title: This Week at Remap
item.content:encoded:
“We need to take a look at these steps. Winter was really rough on a couple of them,” I said.

“I think they just need fresh paint and sealant,“ I said.

“A few boards might need to be replaced,” I added, with what I thought was healthy pessimism.

Anyway, here I am writing this week’s newsletter in one tab. The other contains a shared doc with quotes for a full rebuild on a set of exterior steps. I’m huddled in the downstairs bathroom with the dogs, who can’t stop barking at our handyman and his crew, who are swarming over our deck, knocking out old boards and providing morale-boosting updates like: “It’s a shame all this has rotted. This was really good wood. Prime grade from fifteen years-ago. Eighteen foot boards! You can’t get this anymore. And what you can get? It’s more expensive now, but they probably wouldn’t have even sold it at the lumberyard back then. Too low-quality.”

You think console pricing is bad? Try getting a couple nice two-by-fours.
- Rob

Library-Tier Podcast (Monday, June 8): Remap Detours, “The Bruce Horsnby Appreciator”— It’s been weeks, but I’m still mad about the fact that everyone acted like I was a space alien for knowing where Tupac got most of the music used in “Changes”. An embarrassing moment for everyone but me.

Review (Tuesday, June 9): 007 First Light — Bruno Dias on IO Interactive’s Bond game, which I expect will be a 2000 words of lamentation that Amazon Game Studios couldn’t sink their teeth into the franchise before IOI got hold of it.

Podcast (Wednesday, June 10): “SGF Field Report” — Patrick’s been out in LA covering SGF, getting some hands-on sessions and having conversations that I’m deeply envious of.

Stream (Wednesday, June 10): Motorsport Manger — Chia and Rob had some blessed races when they were streaming from Highground last week for Remap’s Third Anniversary and they’re going to keep up the momentum with help of their Five Star Sponsors and their charismatic, dubiously competent veteran driver.

Feature (Thursday, June 11): Taylor Cocke bought a 3D printer to help with some storage problems, and hoped nothing weird would happen. He was wrong. And yet despite being pulled down into the fever swamps of tabletop wargaming, he’s refused to follow my advice to get into Napoleonics.

Stream (Thursday, June 11): Cleaning Up! — Moving house wasn’t enough for Janet, she’s spent the spring with cleaning and tidying games and so far Cleaning Up! has proven to be the champ. Here’s your chance to experience the vicarious joy of clearing big messes while ignoring the squalor that surrounds you in real life!

Podcast (Friday, June 12): Remap Radio 141 — The gang convenes to discuss SGF, and Rob is BACK IN on Marathon.

Library-Tier Article (Friday, June 12): For this week’s letter series, Rob and Patrick talk about the passing of Bulls broadcaster Stacey King and what makes a great sports commentary team.

If you have any support issues, send details to support@remapradio.com

Rob Zacny (he/him) is a cofounder and partner at Remap. In addition to his work at Remap, he is the host of A More Civilized Age: A Star Wars Podcast and a panelist on Shift+F1, a Formula 1 racing podcast. You can follow his increasingly inactive social media presence on Twitter, and Bluesky.

item.title: What Time Does Rocket League Season 23 Start?
item.content:encoded:
It’s not long now until Rocket League Season 23 starts with a huge and exciting update to the game.

Rocket League Season 23 will be World Cup-themed with free Octane Decals for all 48 participating countries.

And the update will also include multiple quality-of-life improvements, including the ability to choose which side you respawn after a demo.

There will also be the addition of various features, like Hitbox Visualization and Ball Speed Graphs, that players used to have with Bakkesmod before the anti-cheat system was enabled.

Rocket League Season 23 Global Release Times

The new Rocket League season is starting just in time for the start of the World Cup, releasing on Wednesday, June 10.

The exact date and release time for the Rocket League Season 22 update in every major region is as follows:

  • Pacific Time (PDT) – 09:00 June 10
  • Mountain Time (MDT) – 10:00 June 10
  • Central Time (CDT) – 11:00 June 10
  • Eastern Time (EDT) – 12:00 June 10
  • British Summer Time (BST) – 17:00 June 10
  • Eastern European Time (EEST) – 19:00 June 10
  • Moscow Standard Time (MSK) – 19:00 June 10
  • Indian Standard Time (IST) – 21:30 June 10
  • China Standard Time (CST) – 00:00 June 11
  • Japan Standard Time (JST) – 01:00 June 11
  • Australian Eastern Standard Time (AEST) – 02:00 June 11
  • New Zealand Standard Time (NZST) – 04:00 June 11

Once the season launches, Rocket League players will have until July 13, 9:00 AM PT to claim a free Octane Decal and Title for the World Cup country they choose to support.

Then, after the IRL football tournament concludes, players will receive the 47 other World Cup Decals plus a title for the winning country.

Are you excited about the recent updates Psyonix and Epic Games have released for Rocket League? Let us know on the Insider Gaming Discord.

And get ready for an even bigger update, as a future Unreal Engine 6 upgrade for Rocket League has been confirmed.

item.title: NIntendo Direct Announced for Tomorrow Morning
item.content:encoded:
Bringing up the rear of major publishers who seem to be hosting their own events this year in the time of year previously known as E3-season, Nintendo announced that it will be hosting a Nintendo Direct on June 9th, at 10 AM Eastern.

What to know about the Nintendo Direct on June 9

The Nintendo Direct on June 9th will begin at 10 AM Eastern (or 7 am Pacific), and will run for 50 minutes showing both Nintendo Switch, and Nintendo Switch 2 games. It will be followed by another 95 minutes of Nintendo Treehouse Live content showcasing gameplay of some of the titles touched on during the Nintendo Direct. For those who view gameplay reviews as king, you may want to hold your horses during the main direct itself, until knowing if the game will be showing a bunch of gameplay shortly afterwards.

You'll be able to watch the Nintendo Direct on Youtube, Twitch, and via the Nintendo Today app.

With this announcement Nintendo becomes the last of the platform holders to announced their summer announcement event. It will put them a week after Sony held their event on June 2nd which showcased Marvel's Wolverine and God of War Laufey. Xbox took the stage yesterday and showed a mixture of titles as well as a new console-exclusivity plan for some titles like Gears of War: E-Day and Clockwork Revolution, which won't be on PlayStation 5, or Nintendo Switch 2. 

There's also been a richness of independent shows that showcased everything from single developer projects to some of the biggest games in the industry. On Friday was Geoff Keighley's Summer Game Fest which had grand reveals like Resident Evil Code Veronica Remake, and Final Fantasy VII Revelation which will finally be a day and date all platform launch. Following the Xbox showcase was the PC-centric PC Gaming Show which cut down on the antics a bit but really misses Ubisoft and other publishers putting up shows that might compare in that way. It revealed titles like a new Star Trek video game, Stronghold 4, and a Valheim 1.0 release date. 

A longtime lover of speculative fiction, in almost all its forms, Don joined TechRaptor in 2014 on a whim sending in an application as he was looking for… More about Don

item.title: New Halo Remake Trailer Is All Footage From PS5 Pro, Confusing Xbox Fans
item.content:encoded:
The official Halo YouTube channel posted a new story trailer for the upcoming remake, Halo: Campaign Evolved. That in and of itself is no big deal. However, despite being an Xbox-owned franchise, all of the footage seen in the new trailer was captured on a PS5 Pro, a fact that amused, confused, and angered a vast number of gamers online.

On June 8, various official Halo accounts published a new three-minute-long “Cinematic Story Trailer” for Halo: Campaign Evolved that shows off a bunch of moments from the original game, but remade and looking sharper than ever. The trailer looks pretty good, and even if I have mixed feelings about the idea of remaking Halo: Combat Evolved (again), I’m curious how this reimagined spin on the classic will play. But, based on all the online reactions, seemingly the biggest takeaway from the trailer for many was that Xbox captured all the footage on a PS5 Pro, as noted by a simple message seen at the bottom of the video at the start.

A mysterious ring. A lone Spartan.

This is Halo completely rebuilt.

This is Halo: Campaign Evolved.#HaloCE pic.twitter.com/dhac9JzSin

— Halo (@Halo) June 8, 2026

Accounts like Wario64, Charlie Intel, and Paul Tassi highlighted the “Captured On PS5 Pro” note, leading to a lot of reactions. Many were confused as to why Xbox didn’t just use a PC to capture the footage. Sure, the PS5 Pro is more powerful than the Xbox Series X, but it seems odd to highlight that fact.

One possible suggestion floating around for why the trailer may have used PS5 footage is that it was supposed to be at a Sony event. Verge reporter Tom Warren speculated that perhaps this PS5 Pro note and the trailer using footage from Sony’s console is a sign that the trailer was planned to appear at the PlayStation State of Play last week before being pulled. Reliable leaker Rebs Gaming says that was the plan. That might sound like a strangely last-minute change, but keep in mind that it’s reported that the decision for Gears of War: E-Day to skip PS5 happened very recently as Xbox leadership returns to having some games be console exclusive.

Reactions online varied from jokes to fanboy disgust to confusion.

item.title: Xbox Game Pass Lost ‘Millions Of Subscribers’ When It Hiked Prices Last Fall
item.content:encoded:
Xbox Game Pass apparently lost “millions of subscribers” when it raised its prices by 50 percent last fall, according to Xbox’s chief strategy officer, Matthew Ball.

This comes from an interview that The Game Business‘ Chris Dring did with Ball at Summer Game Fest, with the quote being reposted by Geoff Keighley earlier today:

Matthew Ball says Xbox shed “millions of subscribers” when the Gamepass price increased 50 percent in Fall 2025. pic.twitter.com/ESCr1iOEV8

— Geoff Keighley (@geoffkeighley) June 8, 2026

We unfortunately don’t yet have the full context of this quote, nor do we know how many subscribers exactly were lost. In mid-2025, it was thought that the service had over 35 million subscribers, though that number was never officially confirmed. The service was confirmed to be at 34 million subscribers in February of 2024.

Last October, Xbox increased the price of its Game Pass Ultimate plan by 50 percent, alongside a rework of its cheaper Game Pass tiers. This move also took place just a little over a year after it raised prices in 2024. The loss of millions of subscribers explains why new CEO Asha Sharma dropped the price (though not back to 2025 levels) this past April, alongside the announcement that future Call of Duty games would no longer launch into the service.

Xbox Game Pass has had a pretty steep fall from grace over the years, having initially enjoyed wide popularity as a relatively inexpensive service with a large number of good and desirable games on it, but gradually becoming less and less appealing amid slow price increases and a slower drip of alluring games. It’s still getting a lot of really solid new games added to it every month, but has yet to return to the heights of a few years ago when it was essentially the main reason to own an Xbox, largely due to the price.
